meta.hypothesis: For matched 10 keV × N=4096 primaries × DNA_Opt2 physics, the WebGPU Phase A + Phase B (primary + secondary tracking, not including radiolysis chemistry) wall-clock is ≥ 100× faster than Geant4 11.3.0 single-thread on the same machine (M2 Pro).
meta.passBar: speedup_AB_vs_geant4 = T_geant4_median / T_wgsl_phaseAB ≥ 100. Reports the same ratio for Phase A only (upper-bound speedup of fused dispatch alone) and for Phase A+B+IRT (end-to-end pre-DNA pipeline) as additional context, but the pass condition is on Phase A+B (matched scope).

shaderHashesRetrofit.note: Retrofit by tools/retrofit-shader-hashes.mjs — original artifact predated the shaderHashes audit fix (2026-05-12).

summary.headline: Geant4 median 289.1s vs WGSL Phase A+B 635 ms → 455× speedup (physics tracking, matched scope)

rows.scope: WebGPU Phase A (primary tracking only) vs Geant4 (full physics)
rows.note: Upper bound — scopes do not match (Phase A is primary-only, Geant4 simulates primaries + cascade). Reported for context, not pass-bar.

rows.scope: WebGPU Phase A + Phase B (primary + secondary tracking, no chemistry) vs Geant4 (primary + secondary tracking, no chemistry)
WebGPU Phase A + B + IRT chemistry (end-to-end pre-DNA pipeline) vs Geant4 single-thread physics tracking
rows.note: Scopes match for the physics tracking (both include primary + cascade), but WebGPU additionally runs IRT chemistry on CPU. Geant4 here does NOT run chemistry — would require chem6 / dnaChem example. Reported informationally — surfaces that IRT chemistry on CPU is the system bottleneck.
